about_wip: Not yet done. Come back later!
about_koch_intro: The Koch method is a research-backed technique for learning Morse code, developed by German psychologist Ludwig Koch in the 1930s. Unlike traditional approaches that start slowly and speed up, Koch's method trains your brain to recognise characters at full speed from day one.
about_koch_step1_body: Begin by learning only two characters (K and M in the original method) played at your target speed — typically 20 WPM or faster.
about_koch_step2_body: Practice until you can copy those two characters with at least 90% accuracy. Accuracy is the gate, not time.
about_koch_step3_title: Add one character at a time
about_koch_step3_body: Once you hit the accuracy threshold, add one new character to your set and repeat the process. New characters are always introduced at full speed.
about_koch_step4_body: Gradually work through all 40+ Morse characters. Because every character was learned at speed, your brain develops instant recognition rather than counting dots and dashes.
about_koch_why_body: Learning at speed forces your brain to treat each character as a distinct sound pattern rather than a sequence of dots and dashes to decode. This is the same way fluent speakers process spoken language — whole words and sounds, not individual letters sounded out.
about_koch_vs_traditional_body: Start at 5 WPM, gradually increase speed. Result: speed plateaus are common and relearning at higher speeds is often required.
about_koch_vs_koch_body: Start at target speed (20+ WPM), expand character set. Result: learners reach operational speed faster with no speed plateau.

trainer_tooltip_char_wpm: How fast each character is sent. Keep this at your target listening speed.
trainer_tooltip_eff_wpm: Overall pace including the spaces between characters. Lower this to add more thinking time.
trainer_answer_shortcut_tip: Tip: press Space in an empty answer box to start the full session.
trainer_try_again: Try again with a new set
